regions.name: S_occipital_ant
regions.function: Bridges early vision with higher-level object recognition
regions.designImplication: Bridging early vision with object recognition. The brain is transitioning from seeing shapes to identifying what they are.

regions.name: S_oc_middle_and_Lunatus
regions.function: Processes visual contours and shape boundaries
regions.designImplication: Visual contour processing. The brain is tracing boundaries and shape edges in the design. Clean lines and clear boundaries activate this.

regions.name: G_and_S_occipital_inf
regions.function: Early visual processing of objects and faces
regions.designImplication: Early object and face processing. The brain is detecting faces and objects at a pre-conscious level.

regions.name: G_occipital_middle
regions.function: Processing complex visual shapes and object recognition
regions.designImplication: Complex visual shapes are being processed. Higher activation means more intricate visual elements. Good for rich designs, concerning for cluttered ones.

regions.name: Pole_occipital
regions.function: Sharpest central vision processing, what you look at directly
regions.designImplication: Central vision processing. What the viewer looks at most directly. Higher activation means the design's focal point is engaging.

regions.name: S_oc-temp_lat
regions.function: Categorizes visual objects and scenes by type
regions.designImplication: Higher activation here means your design triggers stronger automatic object and scene categorization, helping users process visual content faster.

regions.name: G_oc-temp_lat-fusifor
regions.function: Processes face recognition and identifying familiar visual objects
regions.designImplication: This design triggers strong face processing. Faces in layouts build trust and human connection. If Image A activates this more, its use of faces or face-like elements is more effective at engaging viewers.

regions.name: S_oc_sup_and_transversal
regions.function: Connects visual areas for depth and motion processing
regions.designImplication: Depth and motion processing. The brain is analyzing spatial depth cues in the design.

regions.name: S_collat_transv_post
regions.function: Processes visual scenes and spatial layout
regions.designImplication: Visual scene processing. The brain is analyzing the overall spatial layout of the design as a complete scene.

regions.name: G_cuneus
regions.function: Processes basic visual features like edges and motion
regions.designImplication: Basic visual feature processing (edges, motion, contrast). Higher activation means the design has more visual elements competing for early processing. Good if intentional, bad if chaotic.

regions.name: S_calcarine
regions.function: Receives raw visual information from the eyes
regions.designImplication: Primary visual cortex is processing raw visual information. Higher activation means more visual complexity. Extreme values suggest visual clutter rather than rich content.

regions.name: G_oc-temp_med-Lingual
regions.function: Processes visual word recognition and color perception
regions.designImplication: Visual word recognition is activated. This region responds to readable text and color perception. Higher activation means the text elements are being actively processed, not skimmed over.

regions.name: G_occipital_sup
regions.function: Higher-order visual processing and spatial relationships
regions.designImplication: Higher-order visual processing. Spatial relationships between elements are being analyzed. Higher activation means the layout structure is complex.

regions.name: S_temporal_sup
regions.function: Biological motion and perceiving other people's intentions
regions.designImplication: The brain is reading social intentions. This region tracks biological motion and perceived intentions of people in images. Higher activation means social elements are compelling.

regions.name: S_parieto_occipital
regions.function: Integrates vision with spatial awareness and navigation
regions.designImplication: Integrating vision with spatial navigation. The brain is building a spatial map of the design layout.

regions.name: S_front_middle
regions.function: Executive planning and sustained attention
regions.designImplication: Executive planning and sustained attention. The viewer is maintaining focused attention on the design, possibly planning where to look next.

regions.name: Lat_Fis-ant-Vertical
regions.function: Boundary between motor and language regions
regions.designImplication: Boundary between motor and language regions. Less directly relevant for design evaluation.

regions.name: S_collat_transv_ant
regions.function: Supports face and scene recognition memory
regions.designImplication: Face and scene memory. The brain is encoding faces and scenes for later recall. Higher activation = more memorable faces/scenes.

regions.name: G_front_middle
regions.function: Working memory and cognitive effort — higher activation means more mental work
regions.designImplication: The brain is working hard here. This region handles working memory and cognitive effort. High activation can mean engagement (good) or confusion (bad). Check reward signals to disambiguate.

regions.name: G_parietal_sup
regions.function: Spatial attention — where you direct your gaze
regions.designImplication: Spatial attention is directed. This region controls where the eye goes. Higher activation means the design has a clear visual hierarchy that guides the viewer's gaze.

composites.label: Reward & Aesthetic Pleasure
composites.interpretation: Similar reward & aesthetic pleasure for both images

composites.label: Cognitive Load
composites.interpretation: Image B scores 2% higher. High: Brain working hard — confusion OR deep engagement (check reward level). Low: Easy to process, minimal mental effort required.

composites.label: Visual Processing
composites.interpretation: Similar visual processing for both images

composites.label: Social & Trust Signals
composites.interpretation: Image A scores 3% higher. High: Faces or social cues detected, builds human connection and trust. Low: No social cues, feels impersonal.

composites.label: Directed Attention
composites.interpretation: Similar directed attention for both images

summary: Image A is marginally more effective due to slightly higher social trust signals and deeper engagement of visual recognition systems without increasing cognitive effort.

detailed.winner_reason: Image A is marginally more effective due to slightly higher social trust signals and deeper engagement of visual recognition systems without increasing cognitive effort.
detailed.emotional_impact: Both images elicit similar levels of reward and aesthetic pleasure, indicating comparable positive emotional responses. However, Image A generates slightly more social trust, suggesting it fosters a slightly stronger sense of connection or reliability.
detailed.visual_attention: Both designs capture and hold attention similarly, according to the directed attention signal. However, Image A stimulates more intense processing in specific occipital regions, suggesting its visual elements are more distinctly recognized and articulated by the brain.
detailed.memory_retention: Both designs show similar levels of memorability, suggesting neither has a strong advantage in activating meaning-making circuits or being more inherently memorable than the other.
recommendations: To enhance its strength, identify and amplify the distinct visual features in Image A that are engaging early vision and object recognition, ensuring they contribute to clarity and purpose.
Analyze Image B's content for areas contributing to its slightly higher cognitive load; simplify information presentation or reduce visual density to improve processing ease.

Since memorability is similar for both, consider incorporating more unique or emotionally resonant imagery/narratives into both designs to significantly boost retention and personal meaning for users.
